title: unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access deterministic evidence bank

items.title: unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access: reject the invalid rule
items.prompt: Which statement correctly resolves this CPP misconception? shared_ptr is the safest default for every pointer because more owners always prevent lifetime bugs.
hints: Compare each option with the exact language rule, standard boundary, and observable evidence in the authored draft.

feedback.correct: Correct. Prefer unique_ptr for exclusive ownership, use shared_ptr only for genuine shared lifetime, and break observer cycles with weak_ptr.
feedback.incorrect: That option preserves the documented misconception. Prefer unique_ptr for exclusive ownership, use shared_ptr only for genuine shared lifetime, and break observer cycles with weak_ptr.

criteria.description: Selects the source-aligned CPP correction instead of the documented misconception.

options.text: Prefer unique_ptr for exclusive ownership, use shared_ptr only for genuine shared lifetime, and break observer cycles with weak_ptr.
shared_ptr is the safest default for every pointer because more owners always prevent lifetime bugs.

answer.explanation: Prefer unique_ptr for exclusive ownership, use shared_ptr only for genuine shared lifetime, and break observer cycles with weak_ptr.

items.title: unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access: executable application
items.prompt: Create an integer with std::make_unique, move ownership to a second unique_ptr, and print empty plus the owned value to show the source no longer owns the object.
